other_observers: Mark Peterson, Brandon Percival, Tim Smart;
description: Shortly after 9 am, on a sunny morning, Mark Peterson, Brandon Percival, and I started walking up a low grassy ridge given the moniker ?Pipit Hill.? We had walked about 100 yards when Mark called out, ?Sprague?s Pipit? while pointing roughly to the northeast. I expected to see a bird flying away, but to my surprise, I saw a pipit descending in bouncing, step-wise fashion towards us. As it passed us, we had a mostly side/beneath view with the sun to our backs at about 15-20 feet. It flew on and landed about 100 feet from us. We walked to the area, failed to detect it on the ground, and it flushed upwards, gaining altitude rapidly in a step-wise fashion, and flew > 100 yards away. When it flew, it call several times and was easily heard. It was seen in flight for about 15-25 seconds.  Roughly size and shape of an American Pipit, appearing similar in size, but proportionately a tad shorter tailed. The bird?s underparts were easily viewed when it was close and in pretty good lighting. The face was relatively plain in pattern, excepting a bold white eyering. The background color was somewhat buffy. A series of fine dark streaks formed a pectoral band or necklace across the upper chest. The entire chest was moderately yellowish buff, and this buff extended onto sides. Most of the belly/vent was white or whitish as was the throat. The call was a zjheep-zjheep, with a bit of a squeaky quality. Not as emphatic as that of American Pipit. Neither note of the couplet was emphasized over the other.Behaviors: see descriptionCall: see description belowPlumage: not certain
